title: Jeremiah 27
subtitle: The Yoke of Babylon

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Hananiah Breaks the Yoke

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah to make a yoke ('ol) of straps and crossbars and wear it on his neck (v.2). The yoke is a sign-act addressed to ambassadors from Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon who have gathered in Jerusalem for an anti-Babylonian coalition. The message: every nation must submit to Nebuchadnezzar's yoke. Resistance is futile because God has given "all these lands" to Babylon.

refs.note: Isaiah similarly identifies Assyria as the "rod of my anger" -- the prophetic tradition of naming empires as divine instruments.
"Every authority is established by God" -- Paul's theology of governing authorities follows the same logic Jeremiah applies to Babylon.

notes.note: Wearing a yoke to a diplomatic conference is an outrageous act. Jeremiah becomes a living illustration of his message -- submission to Babylon as God's will.

God claims to have made the earth and given it to whomever he pleases. Nebuchadnezzar's empire is not his own achievement but God's gift -- and the gift is temporary: "until the time for his land comes."
The choice: submit and remain in your land, or resist and be destroyed. Nations that accept the yoke will survive; those that resist will be uprooted.

Calvin develops the theology of divine sovereignty over empires: "God disposes kingdoms as he wills. Neither Nebuchadnezzar's power nor Zedekiah's resistance operates outside divine providence.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Some manuscripts read "Zedekiah" rather than "Jehoiakim" in v.1, which better fits the historical context of vv.3, 12. The date is likely 594-593 BC.
The list of nations -- Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, Sidon -- represents the anti-Babylonian coalition that formed after Nebuchadnezzar faced domestic rebellion in 595-594 BC.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads the yoke sign-act as the boldest prophetic performance in the book. Addressing foreign ambassadors extends Jeremiah's authority beyond Israel to the international sphere -- fulfilling his commission as "prophet to the nations" (1:5). This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies a "theology of imperial mandate" -- God appoints empires for limited terms. Babylon rules by divine permission, not by inherent right, and the permission has an expiration date. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The setting is a diplomatic summit in Jerusalem (c. 594-593 BC) where neighboring kings plot rebellion against Babylon. Jeremiah crashes the conference wearing a wooden yoke on his neck -- the most politically explosive sign-act in the book. His message to the foreign ambassadors is that Nebuchadnezzar rules by God's appointment, and any nation that submits to the yoke will survive. This is political suicide: Jeremiah is telling allied nations to surrender to the enemy.

sections.header: Verses 12-22 -- Word to Zedekiah and the Priests

heb.paragraph: The false prophets claim the temple vessels (kelim) taken to Babylon in 597 BC will "soon be brought back" (v.16). Jeremiah counters: not only will the vessels not return -- the remaining vessels will be taken too (v.22). The temple furnishings symbolize institutional continuity; their removal signals the end of the old order.

refs.note: The final stripping of the temple: bronze pillars broken up, basins, pots, and articles all taken to Babylon -- fulfilling Jeremiah's prophecy precisely.
The vessels from the temple placed in the temple of Nebuchadnezzar's god -- the beginning of the exile.

notes.note: Jeremiah's counsel to Zedekiah: "Put your neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon. Serve him and his people, and you will live." Political submission as the path to survival.

The false prophets' promise of vessel-return is tested: "Let them plead with the LORD that the remaining articles not be taken." If they cannot prevent the second loss, their promise of return is worthless.

Calvin turns the test into a universal principle: "The mark of a true prophet is not eloquence or popularity but effective intercession. A prophet who cannot move God has no authority to speak for God.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The "pillars, the sea, the movable stands" are the major bronze furnishings of Solomon's temple described in 1 Kings 7:15-39 -- the infrastructure of Israel's worship. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom notes that the yoke sign-act addresses three audiences in sequence: foreign nations (vv.1-11), Zedekiah (vv.12-15), and the priests/people (vv.16-22). Each receives the same message in different register. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the vessel dispute as "a conflict over the meaning of institutional symbols." The false prophets cling to the temple vessels as signs of continuity; Jeremiah declares the old order finished. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Jeremiah addresses Zedekiah directly: submit to Babylon and live (v.12). He then confronts the false prophets who promise the rapid return of temple vessels -- a comforting lie. Jeremiah's test: "If they are prophets and have the word of the LORD, let them plead with the LORD" to prevent the remaining vessels from being taken (v.18). The test will fail: in 586 BC, everything is stripped.

lit.note: The yoke sign-act is the most politically dangerous moment in Jeremiah's career -- wearing a yoke to a diplomatic summit where rebellion is being planned.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10 -- God appoints Nebuchadnezzar and disposes kingdoms at will. Prophecy at 9: the yoke as the supreme prophetic sign-act.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Yoke of Babylon

translations.text: Then send word to the kings of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre and Sidon through the envoys who have come to Jerusalem to Zedekiah king of Judah.
Send word to the king of Edom, the king of Moab, the king of the sons of Ammon, the king of Tyre, and the king of Sidon by the hand of the envoys who have come to Jerusalem to Zedekiah king of Judah.

“ ‘ “If, however, any nation or kingdom will not serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon or bow its neck under his yoke, I will punish that nation with the sword, famine and plague, declares the Lord, until I destroy it by his hand.

“‘“But if any nation or kingdom will not serve this Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and put its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon, I will punish that nation with the sword, with famine, and with pestilence, declares the LORD, until I have consumed it by his hand.

debate: Some manuscripts read "Zedekiah" rather than "Jehoiakim" in v.1, which better fits the historical context of vv.3, 12. The date is likely 594-593 BC.…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Yoke of Babylon within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Isaiah similarly identifies Assyria as the "rod of my anger" -- the prophetic tradition of naming empires as divine instruments.
"Every authority is established by God" -- Paul's theology of governing authorities follows the same logic Jeremiah applies to Babylon.
The final stripping of the temple: bronze pillars broken up, basins, pots, and articles all taken to Babylon -- fulfilling Jeremiah's prophecy precisely.
The vessels from the temple placed in the temple of Nebuchadnezzar's god -- the beginning of the exile.

coaching.tip: God commands Jeremiah to wear a yoke — a wooden frame for oxen — and send yoke-messages to neighboring kings. The sign-act says: submit to Babylon or be destroyed. Jeremiah isn't pro-Babylon; he's pro-survival. The prophet distinguishes between God's will and political preference. Sometimes faithfulness means accepting a reality you'd never choose.

The false prophets promise the temple vessels will return quickly from Babylon (v. 16). Jeremiah says they won't — and more will be taken. The test between true and false prophecy here is time: who's right about the future? Jeremiah consistently makes the harder prediction. False prophecy gravitates toward optimism; true prophecy gravitates toward accuracy.

prayer_prompt: As God commands the nations to submit to Babylon's yoke, consider asking God whether there is a difficult authority or circumstance He is asking you to accept rather than resist.
